speaker: interviewer
text: On this one, we'll be able to put the pictures and his [INAUDIBLE].

speaker: interviewer
text: Where do you want me to start?

speaker: interviewer
text: I'll tap you. I'll tap you when.

speaker: interviewee
text: Gera-- or 'Jur-Rah,' I guess-- and eastward was the area that I described where the Germans had machine gunned many of the Holocaust survivors who were trying to make their way eastward into their homes, and was an area that had many, many hundred dead people in it. That was our farthest point east, generally or basically speaking. And then, as you can see, we turned back and went down into Austria, where I had described some of the other things that had happened. The other events that I described, after the war, of course, bear no relationship to the map. This is a picture that was taken by our reconnaissance units. In a previous discussion with Mary, I had thought that this was Buchenwald. But on reflection, I think this is a picture of Ohrdruf. And this was a-- a rather common practice of the Germans prior to burning the bodies. As you can see, what they've done is they've thrown lime over them in order to keep down some of the stench. I don't remember this soldier's name, other than I think it began with an M. This is, after all, 50 years ago, and I never met this soldier after the war.

speaker: interviewer
text: How did you come in possession of this picture?

speaker: interviewee
text: These pictures were taken by our photographer, our division photographer. Or-- or else they were taken by one of the recon men who had a camera with him. And they were then turned over to the-- to the division photographer. And then he reproduced them and distributed them among the senior officers in the division. So there are perhaps-- if other people have saved these, there are perhaps 20 or 30 other people that have a copy of-- of this particular picture.

speaker: interviewee
text: This is a-- another picture taken by the reconnaissance unit at, I believe, Ohrdruf. And that's the same soldier that you see again, the one whose name strikes me as beginning with an M. Something like Madden or Maddingly or something of that sort. But it's a different-- different view, and in the background, you can see excavations for mass burials. And in this particular case, you can see that what they've done is they've built a wooden pyre, and they were burning the bodies on the wooden pyre. And if you look very carefully, you can see bones protruding out of-- out of the pyre. This was not a, quote unquote, 'efficient' system of getting rid of the bodies. But it was the one that was probably utilized to some extent at Ohrdruf. This is a picture of Buchenwald and is part of the scene that I saw as I entered Buchenwald. The soldiers in the background are from various units, probably, of-- of our division. I do not recognize any of these soldiers, and-- And I have to tell you that by the time the war was this far along, most of the people that I knew, like Murray, had either been killed or wounded. And I was only-- I was one of six of the original people left among the [? officer ?] that went with the division-- I mean, with the regiment. I also have-- have to tell you that one of the defenses that you build up when you're in combat is that after a very short period of time, when new people come in, you avoid becoming friendly with them, because you know they're going to be killed. So you just don't get friendly with them. In any event, what this picture does not show-- which is one of the almost indescribable horrors of this situation-- is the smell. These bodies had been dead for many days, and they were beginning to decay. And while this is one small group, there were acres of-- of-- of bodies, and hundreds, hundreds of bodies around. And they were at all stages of-- of decomposition. And they smelled very, very badly. This is a picture that was captured from the Germans and, to me, illustrates the ultimate bestiality of the kind of treatment that they were giving to people that-- that they had captured. This particular man is quite obviously hung by his testicles, and was captured, I believe, in Buchenwald. And although it may have occurred somewhere else, it was simply captured at-- at-- at Buchenwald. This picture was widely distributed throughout our division. Again, it was a copy that was made and given to the senior officers. And there are probably again-- other people that have it. But because of the special bestiality of this picture, many of the enlisted people who saw it wanted copies. And so this picture exists among the people that survived the war in my division and those that were lucky enough to be able to save their effects. I have to say that I have no-- no effects of the war prior to January, because even though I had a camera, it was blown up during the Battle of the Bulge, and I lost my camera and all my films with it. They had some pictures of some of the other things that I had seen during the war. But this particular one is a copy that was given to me, as it was given to other senior officers, and is a very bestial kind of picture.

speaker: interviewee
text: OK. I had earlier mentioned the fact that our wedding had a tie in to the displaced people and-- and former victims of the Holocaust. And it's my wife who knows the details of-- of all of this. I was simply a participant at the time, but she took care of most of the details. So you should question her about it.

speaker: interviewee
text: OK. So please tell us your name and your-- your time of birth and your place of birth. ERLINE HAYES (WIFE): Erline. My maiden name was [? Fleury. ?] I was born in November 19-- November 15, 1919, in Ishpeming, Michigan. How did you become aware of the Holocaust?

speaker: interviewee
text: ERLINE HAYES (WIFE): Really, the-- as I've said-- as he mentioned. The fact that there was the displaced person camp, I didn't associate that too much with the Holocaust. I was working in Red Cross at the time, and we just knew there were people who had been taken out of their homes and-- and grouped together in camps. But the-- the-- the victim I really came across was after we were married and-- and living in Germany and on one of the posts. The wife of one of our neighbors had been a inmate of one of the camps. She had the number tattooed on her arm. And she was-- you could tell-- she was very friendly, but you could tell that she had a-- something in her history that made her sad and made her very not disposed to disclose too much of her history or anything like that. We really didn't know she had been an inmate until somebody-- until it-- it-- it did come out. And then she didn't want to talk too much about it. She just said it was a terrible thing. She could remember being forced to walk through the camp-- she was about 16 at the time-- walk through the camp naked, in front of everybody. And that really destroyed-- girls of 16, they're very modest and-- and shy in the first place. So you can imagine the impression that had on her. And it just was-- it was so sad to think about the things she had to go through.
